name: Ethan Corson
education: Garden City Community College; Washington University in St. Louis (B.S. Political Science, Journalism, and Communication); Washington University School of Law (J.D.)
family: Wife Jenna Brofsky (attorney at Husch Blackwell); sons Isaac and Aiden
whoTheyAre:
Ethan Corson is a 43-year-old Kansas state senator from Fairway running for governor as a Democrat. He grew up in Johnson County, graduated from Shawnee Mission South High School, and played baseball as a left-handed pitcher at Garden City Community College, where he was named an Academic All-American. He went on to earn degrees from Washington University in St. Louis and its law school, then spent seven years practicing labor law in Washington, D.C., at Blake & Uhlig, one of the country's leading union-side law firms.

Corson's career took him through the U.S. Department of Commerce, where he served as Director and Executive Secretariat and later as Senior Advisor to Secretary Penny Pritzker, and then to the International Trade Administration as Chief of Staff. He returned to Kansas in 2019 and led the Kansas Democratic Party as executive director through what the party called its most successful election cycle in a decade, helping elect Governor Laura Kelly and Congresswoman Sharice Davids. He was elected to the Kansas Senate representing District 7 in 2020 and also served as a Dole Institute Fellow.

He lives in Fairway with his wife Jenna Brofsky, an employment attorney at Husch Blackwell who previously served on the Fairway City Council, and their two sons, Isaac and Aiden. Corson is publicly Jewish. His wife grew up attending a Conservative synagogue in a suburb of New York City. He has described his favorite part of every week as making pancakes with his boys before Sunday school.
theirRecord:
In the Kansas Senate, Corson serves as Ranking Minority Member on both the Judiciary and Transportation committees and sits on Assessment and Taxation, Utilities, Legislative Post Audit, and the Russ Jennings Joint Committee on Corrections and Juvenile Justice Oversight. His legislative work has focused on education funding, lowering the cost of living, workforce development, small business support, and rural healthcare access. He has built his campaign around a tagline of "common sense, common ground" and pitched himself as a policy-focused moderate rather than an ideological warrior.

Before the Legislature, Corson had a substantive federal policy resume. His time at the Commerce Department and International Trade Administration gave him executive-branch experience that most state legislators lack, and his years running the Kansas Democratic Party gave him deep institutional knowledge of the state's political machinery. He has crisscrossed roughly 8,000 miles of Kansas campaigning in rural areas where Democrats rarely compete.

Corson has secured endorsements from Governor Laura Kelly, former Governor Kathleen Sebelius, and Congresswoman Sharice Davids, making him the clear establishment favorite in the Democratic primary. Kelly described him as "someone laser focused on the issues Kansans really care about." No significant controversies, legal issues, or public scandals were found in the research reviewed.
whatYouShouldKnow: Corson is the Democratic frontrunner for governor, backed by endorsements from Laura Kelly, Kathleen Sebelius, and Sharice Davids.
He is publicly Jewish and his wife grew up in a Conservative Jewish family. He references attending Sunday school weekly with his sons but no specific synagogue membership has been confirmed.

His professional background is unusually deep for a state legislator: labor law, the U.S. Commerce Department, the International Trade Administration, and executive director of the Kansas Democratic Party.
His 2025 year-end campaign finance report shows $902,641 raised, $375,665 spent, and $526,976 cash on hand. More than 3,000 contributors gave, with about 70 percent donating under $100.
He has no personal loans funding his campaign, and his donor base is broader and more small-dollar-heavy than many other governor candidates in the race.
His campaign platform centers on public schools, lower taxes, rural healthcare access, protecting Kansas farmers from federal tariff damage, and small business growth.
He spent seven years as a labor attorney representing unions at Blake & Uhlig in Washington, D.C., which may be a liability in a general election in a red state.

He attended a church event where he was welcomed by a "Pastor Dallas" to celebrate Black History Month, suggesting he may also attend a Christian congregation in the Kansas City area, though this has not been confirmed.
whereTheyWorship:
Corson is publicly identified as Jewish, and his wife Jenna Brofsky grew up attending a Conservative synagogue in a suburb of New York City. The Kansas City Jewish Chronicle covered his initial Senate candidacy. No specific synagogue membership in Kansas has been confirmed in the public record.

However, Corson has said his favorite part of every week is making pancakes with his sons before Sunday school, and he was photographed being welcomed at a church by a "Pastor Dallas" to celebrate Black History Month. These references suggest the family may also attend a Christian congregation in the Fairway or Kansas City area, but the specific church and denomination have not been identified.

church.details: Corson is publicly Jewish. His wife grew up in a Conservative Jewish family. He also references weekly Sunday school attendance with his sons and has visited at least one Christian church, but no specific congregation has been confirmed.

quotes.text: Kansans should be able to get the care that they need without going broke and without driving for hours to see a doctor. Right now, we have major challenges in our health care system because of the failures in Washington.

If the federal administration had just done nothing, it would have been a good year for most farmers. This is a completely self-inflicted wound that the federal government has put on Kansas farmers and farmers around the country.
We also have to focus on small business. Affordability and small business go hand in hand. This is a small business state, and small business is how we rebuild rural Kansas.
Opportunities for the next generation of Kansans, so they can build a life right here.
work hard, give back whenever you can and always keep your word

campaignFinance.narrative: Corson's 2025 year-end report shows a well-funded, broad-based campaign. More than 3,000 people contributed, with roughly 70 percent giving under $100. He took no personal loans to fund the campaign. He significantly outraised his closest Democratic rival, Cindy Holscher, by more than two to one. The filing shows a grassroots-style donor network that is larger and more diversified than many other candidates in the governor's race.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The full donor list includes 3,000+ contributors. Only a small sample of named donors was extracted from the filing. The complete itemized list is available in the Kansas Ethics Commission filing.
